policy_statement: Systematically use an environmental impact analysis tool (e.g., TOTEM) for construction/renovation projects by the City, Régie, CPAS and SISPs by 2030.
mechanism_description: The City and related public entities will systematically use an environmental impact analysis tool for construction and renovation projects to integrate sustainability and reversibility principles and reduce life-cycle impacts such as embodied emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Project design & procurement decisions → ↓ high-carbon material demand / ↑ reuse & low-carbon substitutes → ↓ supply-chain (embodied) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Public building owners and project teams systematically apply an environmental impact/LCA tool during planning, design, and renovation to compare options and set set (e.g., material selection, structural systems, reuse/deconstruction strategies).  
(2) Mediating process: The tool quantifies life-cycle impacts, steering decisions toward lower-carbon materials, reduced quantities, longer lifetimes, and greater reuse/recyclability, which reduces emissions from extraction, manufacturing (cement/steel), and transport.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower embodied (and sometimes whole-life) GHG emissions associated with construction and renovation, reducing total emissions attributable to the buildings sector supply chain.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced embodied (upfront) GHG emissions from building materials through LCA-informed design and procurement decisions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: LCA tool use mainly affects material and design choices, leading to lower embodied emissions in construction supply chains.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although LCA can also influence resource use (waste, circularity) and sometimes operational energy, the mechanism as stated targets life-cycle impacts such as embodied emissions—i.e., direct GHG mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the tool is used chiefly to reduce material throughput (resource efficiency) without clear GHG prioritization, or if it primarily drives operational energy savings, categorization could shift; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly links tool use to embodied carbon reduction.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is “systematic environmental impact/LCA tool use to reduce embodied emissions” has its primary climate effect in GHG mitigation via reduced embodied carbon, regardless of city context.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether outcomes are mostly from material substitution, reuse, design optimization, or procurement enforcement depends on policy specifics and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the assessment tool is used primarily for non-climate environmental impacts (toxicity, biodiversity) or for circularity targets without demonstrable GHG reductions, the dominant pathway could lean toward Resource Efficiency or Nature-Based Solutions; similarly, if it systematically prioritizes operational energy, mitigation would still apply but via operational (not embodied) emissions.
key_indicators: “impact analysis tool such as TOTEM”; “systematically used”; “By 2030”; “environmental impact of construction/renovation projects”; “integrate the principles of sustainability and reversibility”
co_benefits: Reduced broader environmental impacts (e.g., resource use, waste), improved circularity/reversibility, potential indoor/environmental quality improvements; not specified as primary climate outcomes.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a regulatory-mandate (mandatory use of a tool) or a planning-strategy (design guidance), but the operative mechanism is mandatory assessment/information generation to guide choices, best captured as information-disclosure.
